text: Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery FLS Manual Skills Exam Tips and Tricks We have no financial disclosures.
The Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery FLS provides training and education for residents, fellows, and physicians in the knowledge and surgical skills in laparoscopic surgery.
The FLS Manual Skills Test assesses technical performance in basic laparoscopy.
OB-GYN residents graduating after May thirty-first, twenty-twenty are required to pass this exam in order to achieve board certification.
In this video, we present our tips and tricks for the five technical tasks of the FLS manual skills exam.
Task one, peg transfer.
We recommend using a side grasp to pick up each object and an overhand grasp to receive and place the object onto the peg.
We recommend passing the object directly over the target peg.
We call this the alley-oop method.
The receiving hand uses an overhand grasp to dunk the object over the peg and then moves to the adjacent peg for the next transfer.
This enables the examinee to focus on the transferring hand, which promotes the simultaneous use of both hands and maximizes efficiency.
The surgeon rotates the Maryland Graspers 180 degrees and repeats the object transfer process, using the instruments to pick up and place objects onto pegs.
Task two, precision cutting.
Note, in this video we use the FLS training gauze, which contains two circles.
The actual exam utilizes only one circle.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut directly along the black lines on the practice gauze, simulating real testing conditions.
We recommend approaching the circle from a five o'clock position.
The surgeon partially closes the blades of the scissors and maintains both blades along the incision to achieve a continuous edge on the practice gauze.
The surgeon demonstrates how closing the blades of the scissors during cutting can cause displacement, resulting in jagged edges on the practice gauze.
The grasping hand plays an important role in maintaining proper tension in order to effectively cut.
The surgeon uses the grasping hand to manipulate the practice gauze, adjusting its position to achieve optimal cutting angles.

The surgeon completes the dissection by cutting from the five o'clock to eleven o'clock position in a clockwise direction, then returns to the five o'clock position to finish the dissection in the opposite direction.
We recommend this technique because there is no need to transfer the scissors to the opposite hand during the task.
Task three, ligation loop.
We recommend using the scissors at the beginning of the task to help place the loop and decrease the time required to transfer instruments.
We do not recommend using the ratcheting grasper.
Letting go of it to cinch the knot results in unpredictable manipulation of the appendage.
Task four, suturing with extracorporeal knot tying.
The surgeon grasps the needle while setting it down on a flat surface, preparing for its introduction into the surgical field.
This allows for the predictable orientation of the needle once introduced into the field.
The surgeon pulls on the suture to adjust the orientation of the needle within the surgical field.
The surgeon pinches the top of the incision to align the markings, preparing for precise suturing.
This makes it easier to go through both targets in one motion.
The surgeon ties three square knots in succession, demonstrating the technique for securing the suture.
This allows the examinee to rapidly push down the knots in succession.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal knot tying during the suturing task, manipulating the suture within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the needle through the incision and pulls the suture tail to approximately two centimeters in length, orienting the suture in a C shape for proper alignment.
The right hand grasps the needle end of the suture, positioned one to two centimeters from the needle, preparing for the next step in the suturing process.
The surgeon secures the knot while maintaining the length of the suture tail, ensuring the knot is tightened properly without shortening the tail.
The surgeon transfers the needle end of the suture and positions the suture in a D shape, preparing for the next step in intracorporeal knot tying.
The surgeon repeats the suturing process, positioning the suture in a C shape as the needle is placed through the incision.
We hope you enjoyed this video and we wish you a successful testing experience.
